title: Untitled document.pdf
summaries:
* The Himalayas is a mountain range in Asia separating the plains of the Indian subcontinent from the Tibetan Plateau.
* The range contains the Earth's highest peaks, including Mount Everest, and has more than 100 peaks exceeding elevations of 7,200 metres (23,600 feet) above sea level.
* The range spans five countries: Nepal, India, China, Bhutan, and Pakistan, with sovereignty over the Kashmir region disputed among India, Pakistan, and China.
* It is bordered on the northwest by the Karakoram and Hindu Kush ranges, on the north by the Tibetan Plateau, and on the south by the Indo-Gangetic Plain.
* Major rivers rising in the vicinity include the Indus, the Ganges, and the Tsangpo–Brahmaputra.
* The combined drainage basin of these rivers is home to some 600 million people, while 53 million people live in the Himalayas.

* The combined drainage basin of the Himalayas is home to approximately 600 million people, and 53 million people live in the mountain range itself.
* The range has shaped South Asian and Tibetan cultures, containing many peaks sacred to Hinduism and Buddhism.
* Several summits are off-limits to climbers, including Kangchenjunga (from the Indian side), Gangkhar Puensum, Machapuchare, Nanda Devi, and Kailash.
* The Himalayas were uplifted by the collision of the Indian tectonic plate with the Eurasian plate.
* The mountain range runs west-northwest to east-southeast in an arc 2,400 km (1,500 mi) long.

* The mantle subducted under Eurasia.
* The Himalayan mountain range runs west-northwest to east-southeast in an arc 2,400 km (1,500 mi) long.
* The western anchor, Nanga Parbat, lies just south of the northernmost bend of the Indus river.
* The eastern anchor, Namcha Barwa, lies immediately west of the great bend of the Yarlung Tsangpo River.
* The Indus-Yarlung suture zone separates the Himalayas from the Tibetan plateau, while the rivers also separate the Himalayas from the Karakorams, the Hindu Kush, and the Transhimalaya.
* The range varies in width from 350 km (220 mi) in the west to 151 km (94 mi) in the east.
